era_context: Alexander III continues his consolidation of the former Persian Empire, now pursuing remaining resistance in Bactria and Sogdiana. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests. In India, Chandragupta Maurya is expanding the Mauryan Empire. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin rising as a dominant power. In Greece, the city-states remain under Macedonian hegemony.

events.title: Alexander's Campaign in Bactria and Sogdiana
events.description: Alexander pursued the remnants of Persian resistance into Bactria and Sogdiana (modern Afghanistan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan). These regions, ruled by Bessus (who had proclaimed himself Artaxerxes V), were heavily fortified and inhabited by nomadic and semi-nomadic peoples resistant to Macedonian rule. Alexander's campaigns in 329–327 BCE were among his most difficult, involving siege warfare, guerrilla resistance, and harsh terrain. The conquest of these regions extended Macedonian control to the edges of the known Greek world and brought Alexander into contact with Central Asian peoples and trade routes.
key_figures: Bessus (Satrap of Bactria, self-proclaimed Artaxerxes V)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's campaigns in Bactria and Sogdiana are well-attested in multiple Greek sources. The campaign lasted from 329–327 BCE, with 329 marking the beginning of intensive operations in the region.
cross_references: Led to 327-001 (Completion of Bactrian conquest and marriage to Roxana)

events.title: Execution of Bessus
events.description: Bessus, the satrap of Bactria who had proclaimed himself Artaxerxes V after the death of Darius III, was captured by Alexander's forces in 329 BCE. Bessus was executed—accounts vary on whether he was crucified or flayed alive—as punishment for his role in Darius's assassination and his resistance to Alexander's rule. The execution of Bessus eliminated the last claimant to the Persian throne and consolidated Alexander's position as the legitimate successor to the Achaemenid dynasty.
key_figures: Bessus (Satrap of Bactria, self-proclaimed Artaxerxes V)
events.certainty_note: The capture and execution of Bessus are attested in multiple Greek sources. The date is placed in 329 BCE by modern scholarship, though some sources place it slightly later.
cross_references: Follows from 330-002 (Death of Darius III and succession of Bessus)
Led to 327-001 (Consolidation of Alexander's rule in Central Asia)

events.title: Mauryan Empire Expansion Under Chandragupta
events.description: Chandragupta Maurya continued his rapid expansion of the Mauryan Empire across northern India. By 329 BCE, the Mauryan state had consolidated control over much of the Gangetic plain and was beginning to expand southward and westward. The empire's growth was facilitated by Chandragupta's military innovations, administrative reforms (attributed to Kautilya), and the use of espionage and diplomacy. The Mauryan Empire would become the dominant power in the Indian subcontinent by the early 3rd century BCE.
events.certainty_note: The expansion of the Mauryan Empire is attested in later Sanskrit sources. The precise dating of events in 329 BCE is uncertain; Indian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and synchronisms with Greek accounts.

events.title: Qin State Expansion in Warring States China
events.description: The state of Qin, located in the western part of China, was undergoing rapid military and administrative development under the rule of King Huiwen of Qin (r. 338–311 BCE). Qin pursued aggressive territorial expansion and military reforms, including the adoption of advanced military tactics and the development of a centralized bureaucracy. By 329 BCE, Qin was emerging as a major power in the Warring States system, though it would not achieve hegemony until the late 3rd century BCE.
key_figures: King Huiwen of Qin (Qin ruler, r. 338–311 BCE)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical records for the Warring States period are compiled in texts written centuries later. The Shiji is the primary source, but its chronology for this era is subject to scholarly revision.
cross_references: Contemporary with 329-001 (Alexander's campaigns in Central Asia)
Part of broader Warring States competition

disconfirming_evidence: The method of Bessus's execution is disputed in ancient sources; some accounts describe crucifixion, others flaying, and still others simply execution. The exact date of Bessus's capture and execution is placed in 329 BCE by most modern scholars, but some sources suggest it may have occurred in 328 BCE. The extent and pace of Chandragupta's expansion in 329 BCE is debated; some scholars argue the consolidation was more gradual. The chronology of the Warring States period in China is subject to ongoing scholarly revision, and the precise dating of events in 329 BCE is approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 329 BCE is well-documented for Alexander's campaigns through Greek and later Greco-Roman sources, though these are not contemporary. The sources emphasize Alexander's military achievements and tend to downplay the difficulty and brutality of the campaigns. For India, sources are sparse and compiled centuries later; the chronology is debated. For China, the Shiji provides a framework, but its accuracy for specific years is uncertain. The historical record is heavily skewed toward the Mediterranean, Near East, and parts of Asia; events in sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The victory at Gaugamela in 330 BCE prompted Alexander's pursuit of Darius and subsequent campaigns in Bactria and Sogdiana beginning in 329 BCE
The execution of Bessus consolidated Alexander's control over Bactria and allowed him to consolidate his rule in Central Asia
Chandragupta's expansion of the Mauryan Empire eventually led to conflict with Seleucus I, Alexander's successor in the Near East
